moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn two squares forward, claiming central space and freeing both our bishop and queen for development. This classical move controls the important d5 and f5 squares while opening lines for our pieces. Our plan is to build a strong center and develop quickly, following fundamental opening principles. This move has been the choice of countless world champions because it immediately puts pressure on Black and offers rich tactical and strategic possibilities.

Our opponent plays the provocative Alekhine Defense, immediately attacking our central pawn with the knight rather than mirroring with e5. This hypermodern approach invites us to advance our pawns and build a big center, with Black planning to undermine it later. The knight on f6 also prevents us from playing the Scholar's Mate threats, but more importantly, it's following Alekhine's philosophy of inducing our pawns forward where they might become targets. We need to decide whether to defend the e4 pawn or advance it aggressively.

We're advancing our pawn to chase the knight away, gaining space in the center and following the main line of the Alekhine Defense. This move gives us a strong pawn duo on e5 and (eventually) d4, controlling key central squares like d6, f6, c5, and f5. Our advance also opens up the diagonal for our light-squared bishop on f1. The downside is that we're committing to a pawn structure that Black hopes to attack later, but for now we're seizing the initiative and forcing Black's pieces to less active squares.

Black retreats the knight to the central d5 square, where it's well-placed but will likely face further harassment from our pawns. From d5, the knight controls important squares like c7, e7, f6, f4, e3, and c3, maintaining some central influence despite being pushed around. Our opponent is following typical Alekhine Defense strategy - allowing us to build a big pawn center while keeping pieces actively placed to strike back later. We'll likely continue with c4 next to kick this knight again, but Black has achieved the goal of making us advance our pawns early in the game.
